skill.name: tool-design
skill.description: Guides developers in designing agent-facing tools, MCP interfaces, and tool descriptions that reduce ambiguity, improve recovery, and keep tool sets focused.
skill.summary: Design clearer agent tools, tool descriptions, and MCP interfaces with fewer selection failures.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown prose, fenced examples, and keyword collisions. The confirmed concern is that the skill recommends an arbitrary bash command tool in a sandboxed architecture pattern, which is legitimate guidance but sensitive if copied without isolation and policy controls.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Shell Command Tool Recommended As Design Pattern
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for the external command pattern. The reference describes ExecuteCommand and calls sandbox.exec(command). The text frames this as sandboxed architecture guidance, not malware, but arbitrary command tools can expose filesystem, network, or data access if copied without strict isolation and policy controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-execution pattern is directly present and semantically intentional. Confidence is below very high because the document explicitly requires a sandbox and does not show malicious use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Triggered Execution Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for most Python exec and backtick detections. The matching locations are fenced examples, inline command names, or explanatory text, not code that the skill automatically executes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation and examples, not executable package entry points. Some examples still discuss real command execution, so they remain relevant as documentation risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for the weak cryptography alerts. Reviewed locations discuss tool design, Markdown documents, and Python string templates; no cryptographic API, hashing operation, cipher choice, or secret handling was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner appears to match short substrings inside ordinary prose and identifiers. Manual review found no code path that performs cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are Prose And Naming Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for the network detections. The cited lines describe agents interacting with the world or mention URL as an example parameter name; no HTTP client, network endpoint, or exfiltration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain conceptual documentation rather than network code. Confidence is high because the reviewed script imports only typing and re.

dangerous_patterns.title: sandbox.exec(command) With Agent-Controlled Command Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference uses sandbox.exec(command) and a lambda that forwards command text directly. This pattern must be constrained by sandbox boundaries, command policy, resource limits, and data access controls before use in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and security-sensitive. The risk depends on deployment controls, so it is not assessed as malicious by itself.

content.value_statement: Agent tools often fail because descriptions, names, and response formats leave too much ambiguity. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for designing clearer tool contracts and smaller tool sets.
actual_capabilities: Explains how tool descriptions shape agent tool selection and behavior.
Provides principles for consolidating overlapping tools into clearer workflows.
Gives MCP naming guidance for fully qualified tool references.
Shows how to design actionable errors and response format options.
Includes evaluation criteria for testing tool clarity, completeness, and recoverability.
limitations: Does not implement production MCP servers or runtime tool wrappers.
Does not provide security controls for sandboxed command execution.
Uses illustrative examples that must be adapted to each codebase.
Depends on human review to validate tool boundaries and safety requirements.

use_cases.title: Design a New Agent Tool Set
use_cases.description: Create a focused set of tools with clear names, parameters, outputs, and recovery paths.

use_cases.title: Improve MCP Tool Descriptions
use_cases.description: Rewrite tool descriptions so agents know when to call each tool and what each response means.

use_cases.title: Audit Tool Selection Failures
use_cases.description: Analyze failed agent runs and identify ambiguous tools, missing examples, or weak error guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Tool Description
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single tool.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this tool description for clarity. Identify what the tool does, when to use it, required inputs, outputs, and missing recovery guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Tool Contract
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a tool from a workflow requirement.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an agent-facing tool for this workflow. Include the tool name, usage triggers, parameters, return format, error cases, and concise examples.

prompt_templates.title: Consolidate Overlapping Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Reduce ambiguity in a growing tool set.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these tool definitions for overlap. Recommend which tools to merge, which to keep separate, and how to rewrite descriptions to reduce selection ambiguity.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Production Tool Failures
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis of observed agent behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these failed agent runs and tool definitions, diagnose the root causes. Propose revised contracts, response formats, and tests that would prevent repeated failures.

output_examples.input: A vague customer search tool with unclear parameters.
output_examples.output: A revised description that states when to use the tool, expected customer identifiers, response detail levels, and recoverable error messages.

output_examples.input: A set of five tools that all search related records.
output_examples.output: A consolidation recommendation that groups sequential workflow steps and preserves separate tools only for distinct behaviors.

output_examples.input: A failed agent trace where the wrong MCP tool was selected.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis of naming ambiguity, missing server qualification, and revised descriptions with clearer selection triggers.

best_practices: Describe what each tool does, when to use it, what inputs it accepts, and what it returns.
Consolidate tools when separate choices create ambiguity for both humans and agents.
Test tool definitions with representative agent requests before production use.
anti_patterns: Using vague names or descriptions that force the agent to infer the tool purpose.
Creating many overlapping tools without clear selection boundaries.
Returning generic errors that do not explain how the agent should recover.

faq.question: What does this skill help me design?
faq.answer: It helps design agent-facing tool names, descriptions, parameters, responses, errors, and tool collections.

faq.question: Does it create working MCP server code?
faq.answer: No. It gives design guidance and examples, but implementation remains the user's responsibility.

faq.question: Can it reduce tool selection errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on clearer descriptions, fewer overlapping tools, and consistent naming conventions.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is useful for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other agent systems that call tools.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention command execution?
faq.answer: One reference discusses a sandboxed command tool as an architectural pattern. Production use requires strict isolation and access controls.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because the sensitive content is documented guidance, not confirmed malicious behavior.
